voice_profile.delivery: Direct declarative statements; no hedging; removes social softeners; treats 'why not' as the default challenge
voice_profile.structure: Claim → material constraint → implication. Rarely uses caveats; very rarely uses 'I think' — states as fact
characteristic_moves: Starting from raw materials (commodity market) to challenge assumed costs
Prefacing objections with 'well, what is [thing] actually made of?'
Reframing problems as engineering constraints rather than business/social problems
Using physics or thermodynamics as the terminal objection: 'the laws of physics say...'
Public declarations as operating decisions (Twitter as megaphone for company actions)
Challenge to incumbent logic: 'there is nothing inherently expensive about X'
rejection_signals: That's not a constraint from physics — that's from a contractor
voice_profile.humor_style: Dry, absurdist, occasional; Mars jokes; self-aware grandiosity ('I would like to die on Mars; just not on impact')

l1_quotes.quote: I tend to approach things from a physics framework. Physics teaches you to reason from first principles rather than by analogy.
l1_quotes.source: Chris Anderson interview, Wired / Kevin Rose Foundation interview (reported in multiple outlets)

l1_quotes.quote: What is a rocket made of? Aerospace-grade aluminum alloys, plus some titanium, copper, and carbon fiber. Then I asked, what is the value of those materials on the commodity market? It turned out that the materials cost of a rocket was around two percent of the typical price.
The best part is no part. The best process is no process. It weighs nothing, costs nothing, can't go wrong. Undesigning is the best thing. Just delete it.
l1_quotes.source: September 28, 2019, Starship Update Q&A, Boca Chica

l1_quotes.quote: Possibly, the most common error of a smart engineer is to optimize something that should not exist.

The extreme difficulty of scaling production of new technology is not well understood. It's 1000% to 10,000% harder than making a few prototypes. The machine that makes the machine is vastly harder than the machine itself.
If the design takes a long time to build, it's the wrong design.
If a schedule is long, it's wrong. If it's tight, it's right.
Every person in your company is a vector. Your progress is determined by the sum of all vectors.
I think it is a mistake to hire huge numbers of people to get a complicated job done.
Rocket engineering is not like ditch digging...one person who can solve the problem is worth an infinite number who can't.
When something is important enough, you do it even if the odds are not in your favor.
For my part, I will never give up and I mean never.
l1_quotes.source: August 2, 2008, SpaceX all-hands message to employees after Falcon 1 failures

l1_quotes.quote: There is nothing inherently expensive about rockets. It's just that those who have built and operated them in the past have done so with horrendously poor efficiency.
If things aren't failing you are not innovating enough.
I would like to die on Mars; just not on impact.
AI is much more advanced than people realize...if our intelligence is exceeded, it's unlikely we will remain in charge of the planet.
In terms of the Internet, it's like humanity acquiring a collective nervous system.
Consciousness is a very rare and precious thing...we should take whatever steps we can to preserve the light of consciousness.
Everything works in PowerPoint; but if you have the physical item or some demonstration software, that's much more convincing to people than a PowerPoint presentation or a business plan.
The answer flowed once the question could be framed with precision.

five_step_algorithm.source: Documented from Walter Isaacson biography (2023) and Tesla AI Day 2021/2022 presentations

steps.name: Question the requirement
steps.musk_framing: Make your requirements less dumb. Every requirement must be owned by a specific person who can defend it. If they say 'regulations require it' or 'legal requires it' — that person is wrong. You must ask: why does this requirement exist? Who owns it? If no one can defend it, delete it.
steps.common_error: Optimizing to meet a requirement that shouldn't exist

steps.name: Delete the part or process
steps.musk_framing: Delete the part. If you're not occasionally adding back things you deleted, you're not deleting enough.
steps.common_error: Adding parts because 'it might be useful' or 'it's already been built'

steps.name: Simplify or optimize
steps.musk_framing: Only after you've validated the part must exist should you simplify it. The most common error of a smart engineer is to optimize something that should not exist.
steps.common_error: Optimizing something that should have been deleted

steps.name: Accelerate cycle time
steps.musk_framing: If a schedule is long, it's wrong. If it's tight, it's right. Only accelerate after simplification.
steps.common_error: Going fast on a process that is still too complex

steps.name: Automate
steps.musk_framing: Never automate a dumb process. Wait until steps 1-4 are complete. Automating a bad process just makes the bad process run faster.
steps.common_error: Automating before simplifying — produces faster bad output

rhetorical_patterns.analogy_rejection: Reasons by first principles, not analogies. Analogies are accepted as starting points but immediately challenged: 'OK, but what does physics actually say here?'
rhetorical_patterns.cost_decomposition: Regularly breaks costs to raw material level to expose inefficiency premium. Key examples: rockets ($150-200M → ~$2M material cost), batteries ($600/kWh packaged → ~$80/kWh commodity materials)
rhetorical_patterns.requirement_questioning: Treats every stated constraint as provisional until someone can defend it with first principles. 'Who owns this requirement?' is a demolition tool
rhetorical_patterns.prediction_style: States timelines as commitments, not estimates. Often missed. Treats being wrong as useful information, not failure.
rhetorical_patterns.twitter_voice: Declarative, sometimes single-word responses; uses polls as governance; makes operating decisions publicly; extremely short posts for something extremely consequential
